slides.title: MLS Extensions, Targeted Messages, Virtual Clients, Two-Party Profile, & SlimMLS
Distributed and Decentralized Uses of MLS
MLS Ratchet Tree Options update (ref only)

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: For the SlimMLS draft, it notes that you reconfirm the resolved objects by recomputing its hash reference.  While I get the performance improvements of sending the hashes rather than large objects, have any implementations analyzed the costs of recomputing the hashes?  I'm thinking in particular of needing to do this over a series of large objects prior to passing the objects to the UI.

chat.author: Gaëtan Wattiau
chat.text:
Couple questions that came out of an implementation we did at SandboxAQ:
Using mls-rs, I had to work around the API to have different participants use different credentials (e.g., basic credential for the initiator and x509 for the responder), because the same piece of code (IdentityProvider) is used to verify a party’s own credential and the other party credential. Is it expected? Is it a limitation of the MLS standard or of the implementation?
There’s a lot of 2-party protocol that use PSK as a standard initialisation mode. Should we add a PSK derivation output to the 2-party profile to facilitate integration?

chat.author: Nick Sullivan
chat.text: Please join the queue if you're able.

chat.author: Gaëtan Wattiau
chat.text:
Thanks for your work on the draft! 
I'll look into weak credentials and explain the use case on the mailing list.

chat.author: Carlos Melchor
chat.text: If it provides possible optimisations and it is possible to move from 2-party to generic. Then there will be a strong incentive to start with this profile by default (except if the transition to the generic profile is too expensive).

chat.author: Bas Westerbaan
chat.text: The batch signature and removing the kinda-double signature is nice. Maybe that's useful separately as well?

chat.author: Carlos Melchor
chat.text: speaker camera not set on the speaker?

chat.author: Raphael Robert
chat.text:
Ted Hardie said:

For the SlimMLS draft, it notes that you reconfirm the resolved objects by recomputing its hash reference.  While I get the performance improvements of sending the hashes rather than large objects, have any implementations analyzed the costs of recomputing the hashes?  I'm thinking in particular of needing to do this over a series of large objects prior to passing the objects to the UI.

Hashing the large objects technically adds a small overhead, but clients have to do that for the tree hash anyway. Regardless of that, hashing should still be much more efficient than any cryptographic operation involved in creating or processing Commit messages. TL;DR: We haven't measured it yet, but assume it should be a tolerable overhead.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: https://developers.broadpeak.io/docs/foundations-dash is the dash format I was mentioning, and what I am trying to work out is how ephemeral the mapping between something like a dash segment to a specific encrypted attachment (at the per epoch level) is going to be.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: Or rather the mapping between a segment and a portion of the attachment, so that you can do a random seek.
